title: Robust fluctuating intertwined charge stripes in the Emery model
authors: Rong Zhang, Sijia Zhao, Hong-Chen Jiang, Brian Moritz, Edwin W. Huang, Thomas P. Devereaux
abstract: The single-band Hubbard model is one of the most extensively studied models in condensed matter physics, giving rise to intertwined spin and charge stripes that coexist with, or lie in the vicinity of, superconductivity in the phase diagram. However, whether the low energy physics of the single-band Hubbard model is fully equivalent to the multi-band (multi-orbital) Emery model remains an unsettled question. While the intertwined stripes and nematicity have been studied in the single-band Hubbard model, a comprehensive picture in the Emery model is lacking. In this paper, we focus on the less investigated intertwined charge stripes using complementary density matrix renormalization group (DMRG) and determinant quantum Monte Carlo (DQMC) techniques. Our ground state DMRG confirms the presence of the oxygen-centered charge stripes at a reduced amplitude in the Emery model parameter regime widely used in the study of intertwined stripes. Close analysis of the oxygen orbital structure of the static charge correlation function from DQMC reveals the charge stripe pattern in real-space, showcasing stronger charge density modulation on $p$-orbitals pointing along ``the rivers of charge'', consistent with DMRG. For the parameter set with the largest fermion signs, we managed to reach a temperature where the system first demonstrated tendencies to form purely unidirectional spin and charge stripes, and the $B_{1g}$ component becomes dominant in the bond-charge nematic susceptibility. This observation correlates with the doping dependence of the kinetic energy anisotropy, suggesting a close relation between the nematicity and charge stripes in the Emery model.
summary: This paper uses DMRG and DQMC to show that the Emery model supports robust, intertwined charge and spin stripes. It specifically highlights how oxygen orbital structure contributes to charge modulation and how temperature-driven symmetry crossovers link nematicity to stripe formation.
main_problem: The study investigates whether the low-energy physics of the single-band Hubbard model is equivalent to the multi-band Emery model and explores the nature of intertwined spin and charge stripes and nematicity within the Emery model.
main_result: The researchers confirm the presence of oxygen-centered charge stripes and demonstrate that cooling the system triggers a crossover from B2g to B1g symmetry-breaking tendencies, linking nematicity to charge stripes.
method: The study utilizes complementary Density Matrix Renormalization Group (DMRG) for ground-state simulations and Determinant Quantum Monte Carlo (DQMC) for finite-temperature properties.
model_or_system: The Emery model, a three-band Hubbard model representing the CuO2 plane, consisting of copper-centered d-orbitals and oxygen-centered p-orbitals.
key_observables: Static charge and spin correlation functions, bond-charge nematic susceptibilities (B1g and B2g), and kinetic energy anisotropy.
important_parameters: Parameters include hopping integrals (tpd, tpp), Hubbard repulsions (Udd, Upp), charge transfer energy (pd), and hole doping levels.
main_assumptions: The DQMC simulations are limited by the fermion sign problem, which restricts the exploration of very low temperatures or high interaction strengths.
figures_summary: Figures show real-space DQMC patterns of spin and charge stripes, momentum-space correlations illustrating the Yamada relation, and the temperature evolution of symmetry-breaking modes.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of model equivalence, describes the Emery model parameters, presents DMRG ground-state results, details DQMC finite-temperature findings regarding stripe evolution and nematicity, and concludes with the connection between orbital physics and stripe directionality.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Doping dependence of DMRG spin and charge stripes Left (a, c, e): staggered spin-spin correlations on copper d orbitals for increasing hole dopings, in reference to the d orbital at (0, 0). Right (b, d, f): charge density fluctuation profiles ⟨ni,α⟩−⟨nα⟩, α = d, px, py, for each orbital plotted at their positions. The plotting convention and three-band parameters are the same as in Fig. S5. The doping dependence of the spin and charge stripe period is consistent with that in Fig. S5. The charge density fluctuation on py is generally stronger than that on px. Calculations were performed on a 24 × 4 or 32 × 4 cylinders, with the center 16 × 4 rungs shown here.

Figure 1 shows the staggered spin correlation on cop- per (left) and spatial fluctuations of the charge density profile (right) from a DMRG calculation of the inner 16 rungs of a 24 × 4 (32 × 4 for 1/8 doping) cylinder at Udd = 6 eV, Upp = 0, ∆pd = 3 eV. The DMRG staggered spin correlations show a shorter spin stripe period with increasing hole doping. Charge accumulates near the AFM domain wall, while being depleted near the center of the cylinder. In panels (b, d, f), the charge densities on d, px, py orbitals vary concomitantly with a common periodicity, locking to half of the spin stripe period. This charge modulation is strongest on the py orbital and weakest on the d orbitals....

FIG. 2. Real space patterns of DQMC spin and charge correlation functions (a) Staggered spin correlation function χ∗ s,ij(ω = 0), showing fluctuating spin stripe domains staggered across the length of the cylinder and running along the vertical direction. (b) Static charge correlation function between px orbitals χxx c (ω = 0), showing a tendency to form horizontal stripes due to the local perturbation on the x orbital. (c) Static charge correlation function between px and py orbitals χxy c (ω = 0), showing a tendency to form stripes along the vertical direction due to the lattice geometry, where “rivers of charge” would be pinned to the spin stripe domain walls. FIG. 3. Momentum space DQMC spin and charge correlation functions on a 16 × 4 cluster with Upp = 0, Udd = 8.5 eV (a) Static spin correlation function χs(q, ω = 0). (b) Fourier transform of the static oxygen charge correlation function with respect to py, χyy c (q, ω = 0) + χxy c (q, ω = 0), In (a) and (b), the markers represent the data, and solid lines are fitted periodic double Lorentzian functions as defined in Ref. [23]. (c) The fitted modulation wavevectors of the spin density wave δSDW and half of the charge density wave δCDW/2 at each doping p, showing a Yamada-like relation. Similar plots for other model parameters are shown in Figs. S17 and S18.

FIG. 4. Temperature evolution of staggered spin-spin (a, b, c) and connected px −py-orbital charge-charge correlation (d, e, f) from a DQMC simulation on 8 × 8 system The shape of the spin stripe domain in the correlation function gradually evolves from square-like at high temperatures in (a) towards the diamond-like shape in (e). The diamond- like domain wall is the momentum space superposition of horizontal and vertical spin stripes extending in the direction of the Cu-O bonds, while the square-like domain wall is the momentum space superposition of the spin stripes in the two diagonal directions.
